title: Assistive Devices: Safe Use of Canes, Walkers, and Crutches
subtitle: The nurse teaches safe use of canes, walkers, and crutches — cane on the strong side, weight on the hands (not the armpits) with crutches, and 'up with the good, down with the bad' on stairs.

patient.name: Assistive Devices
patient.admitDate: The nurse teaches safe, fall-preventing use of canes, walkers, and crutches.

vitals.temp: Up with good, down with bad

labs.v: Advance ~a step; weak then strong leg.
Nonskid shoes, intact tips, clear path.

nursesNote: The nurse teaches safe use of mobility aids. A cane is held on the strong (unaffected) side, advanced together with the weak leg, then the strong leg follows; the handle reaches the wrist crease with the elbow flexed about 15-30 degrees. With a walker, all four legs are placed on the floor before the patient steps, the device is advanced about a step's length, and the weak leg moves before the strong one while the patient stays inside the frame. Crutch weight is borne on the hands, never the axillae, with the pads about two finger-widths below the armpits to avoid nerve injury. On stairs the rule is 'up with the good, down with the bad': the strong leg leads going up, and the crutches with the weak leg lead going down. The nurse reinforces nonskid shoes, intact rubber tips, and a clear, clutter-free path to prevent falls.
providerOrders: Hold the cane on the strong side and advance it with the weak leg
Bear crutch weight on the hands, not the axillae
Place all walker legs on the floor before stepping
On stairs: lead up with the strong leg, down with the weak leg
Check footwear, rubber tips, and a clear path before ambulating

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about assistive devices?

opts.text: A cane is held on the strong (unaffected) side
Crutch weight is borne on the hands, not the axillae
All four walker legs are on the floor before stepping forward
Crutch pads should rest snugly up in the armpits
Elbows are flexed about 15-30 degrees with a cane or walker
The weak leg moves forward with the cane
Bearing weight on the axillae can damage nerves under the arm
Going up stairs, the strong leg leads ('up with the good')
Going down stairs, the crutches and weak leg go first
The cane is advanced together with the strong leg
Intact rubber tips and proper footwear improve stability
The walker is advanced about a step's length, not too far

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the steps to walk with a cane

opts.text: Place the cane on the strong side, tip a few inches out
Advance the cane and the weak leg together
Bear weight through the cane and hands
Step forward with the strong leg
Make sure balance is steady before the next step
Holding the cane on the strong side
Resting crutch pads tightly in the armpits
Flexing the elbows about 15-30 degrees
Advancing the cane with the strong leg
Putting all walker legs down before stepping
Leading with the strong leg going up stairs
Bearing body weight on the hands with crutches
Leaning out over the front of the walker

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Order going UP stairs with crutches
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stand in the tripod position near the step
Bear weight on the hands through the crutches
Step up with the strong leg first
Bring the crutches and weak leg up to that step
Steady balance before the next step
Holding the cane on the unaffected side
Bearing weight on the axillae with crutches
Leading with the strong leg going up stairs
Leading down stairs with the crutches and weak leg
Using a walker with worn or missing rubber tips
Wearing well-fitting nonskid shoes
Sliding the crutch pads up into the armpits
Keeping the path clear of rugs and clutter

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Basic Care and Comfort, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
